title: Promoting Access to Mortgage Credit

flags.justification: Coordinates deregulatory action across the Federal Reserve, CFPB, NCUA, FDIC, OCC, HUD, VA, FHFA, and USDA -- independent regulatory agencies with statutorily protected independence -- all directed by a single executive order toward a unified deregulatory policy outcome, testing the boundary of executive direction over agencies Congress designed to be independent of direct presidential control.

Virtually every substantive directive throughout the order uses 'shall consider' rather than 'shall' (e.g., Sec 2(a), Sec 4(a), Sec 9(a)), creating a structure where agencies can claim compliance merely by considering the listed actions without ever implementing them, while political pressure to actually implement remains.

Bundles origination/ATR-QM rulemaking reform (Sec 2), HMDA data-collection modernization (Sec 3), bank capital/liquidity-framework changes (Sec 4), construction-lending guidance (Sec 5), appraisal modernization (Sec 6), digital-mortgage-document modernization (Sec 7), servicing-supervision changes (Sec 8), an enforcement-leniency policy (Sec 9), and licensing-requirement elimination (Sec 10) -- ten distinct regulatory domains spanning at least eight independent agencies, one of the most extensively bundled orders in this entire corpus.
This order addresses regulatory/supervisory policy considerations rather than establishing a new enforcement standard against private parties.

Sec 9(a)'s directive to 'discourage... civil monetary penalties, except where the underlying violations are willful, knowing, or reckless' combined with 'correction-first supervisory treatment' (Sec 9) for consumer financial law violations reduces the deterrent effect of routine compliance monitoring, potentially incentivizing lenders to treat non-willful compliance failures as lower-priority given reduced penalty exposure.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing deregulatory policy framework.

Directing 'independent' financial regulatory agencies (the Federal Reserve, FDIC, NCUA, OCC, CFPB) -- which Congress structured with tenure protections and independence from direct presidential removal specifically to insulate financial regulation from short-term political pressure -- toward a unified deregulatory outcome via a single executive order tests and pushes against that statutory independence, even though the order's 'consider' framing provides a degree of formal deniability.
Direct regulatory-agency-directed mechanism; the order's core mechanism does not depend on private third-party voluntary action.

Simultaneously loosening ATR/QM underwriting standards (Sec 2), capital/risk-weight requirements (Sec 4), appraisal requirements (Sec 6), and enforcement posture (Sec 9) for community and smaller banks' mortgage lending, if implemented in combination, recreates several of the specific regulatory-gap conditions (weakened underwriting standards, reduced capital buffers, lenient enforcement) that contributed to the 2008 mortgage crisis that originally prompted the Dodd-Frank reforms this order's Sec 1 criticizes -- a systemic-risk tension the order does not acknowledge or analyze despite bundling nearly every major post-2008 mortgage safeguard into a single deregulatory review.

At least eight agencies (Federal Reserve, CFPB, NCUA, FDIC, OCC, HUD, VA, FHFA, USDA) are independently directed to 'consider' overlapping and interlocking regulatory changes across capital, underwriting, servicing, and appraisal rules without any single coordinating body or lead agency established by this order.

The order is substantially organized around creating tailored exemptions and reduced requirements specifically for 'community banks' and 'smaller banks' (defined as under $100 billion in assets) across nearly every section, representing an extensive size-based exemption architecture.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=9 (F1,F2,F3,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=2 (F4,F6). raw=F1(1)+F2(1)+F3(2)+F5(1)+F7(1)+F8(0)+F9(2)+F10(1)+F11(1)=10. max=18. pct=10/18=55.56%. Notable: one of the most extensively bundled orders in the corpus (10 sections, 8+ independent agencies), directing nominally independent financial regulators toward a unified deregulatory outcome via 'consider' language.

title: Removing Regulatory Barriers to Affordable Home Construction

flags.justification: CEQ Chairman and Advisory Council on Historic Preservation Chairman given authority to develop guidance 'maximally exempting' housing construction from NEPA and NHPA Section 106 review (Sec 3), a sweeping direction toward the maximum permissible deregulatory outcome under these statutes.

The order's directives, while extensive, are relatively transparent about their deregulatory purpose and specific targeted regulations (e.g., naming the Construction General Permit, specific HUD/USDA programs).

Bundles Clean Water Act/wetlands permitting review (Sec 2(a)), federal housing-program elimination review across Commerce/HUD/Transportation/FHFA (Sec 2(b)), energy-efficiency-standard rollback across Agriculture/HUD/Energy/FHFA (Sec 2(c)), NEPA categorical-exclusion maximization (Sec 3(a)), NHPA Section 106 burden reduction (Sec 3(b)), state/local best-practices development (Sec 4), and Opportunity Zone/New Markets Tax Credit coordination (Sec 5) across at least eleven federal agencies/offices in one order.
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism; this order addresses federal regulatory/guidance review.

Directing agencies to 'maximally exempt' housing construction from NEPA and NHPA review (Sec 3(a)-(b)) using an outcome-oriented 'maximize deregulation' instruction rather than a case-by-case environmental/historic-impact assessment standard could incentivize categorical exclusions that do not actually correlate with genuinely low-impact projects, undermining the individualized review these statutes are designed to provide.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing deregulatory policy framework.

Sec 3(a)-(b) directs CEQ and the Advisory Council on Historic Preservation to develop guidance maximizing categorical exclusions from NEPA and reducing NHPA Section 106 review burdens specifically to reduce the scope of environmental and historic-preservation oversight that would otherwise apply to housing/infrastructure projects.

Sec 4's 'regulatory best practices for State and local governments' (developed by HUD) depend entirely on states' and localities' voluntary adoption, since the federal government cannot directly mandate state/local zoning, permitting timelines, or building-code decisions.

Maximally exempting housing construction from wetlands/stormwater (Sec 2(a)), NEPA (Sec 3(a)), and historic-preservation (Sec 3(b)) review simultaneously, combined with rolling back energy-efficiency standards for manufactured/HUD/USDA-financed housing (Sec 2(c)), could produce cumulative environmental, flood-risk, and long-term-utility-cost effects for new housing stock that the order does not analyze in combination, even though each individual rollback may be independently justifiable.

Army Corps of Engineers, EPA, Commerce, HUD, Transportation, FHFA, Agriculture, Energy, CEQ, ACHP, and Treasury are all independently tasked with overlapping deregulatory reviews across their own regulatory domains without a single coordinating lead beyond HUD's Sec 4 role for state/local best practices.

The order's central mechanism across nearly every section is the creation or expansion of exemptions from existing environmental, historic-preservation, energy-efficiency, and land-use requirements specifically for housing construction -- one of the most extensive and centrally-organized exemption architectures in this entire corpus, spanning federal environmental law (NEPA, CWA), historic preservation law (NHPA), and energy-efficiency standards simultaneously.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=9 (F1,F2,F3,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=2 (F4,F6). raw=F1(1)+F2(0)+F3(2)+F5(1)+F7(1)+F8(1)+F9(1)+F10(1)+F11(2)=10. max=18. pct=10/18=55.56%. Notable: paired with EO14393 (same date), together forming a two-order, ~19-agency, ~17-section deregulatory bundling of financial and land-use/environmental regulation in a single day -- see pattern log.

title: Establishing the Task Force To Eliminate Fraud

flags.justification: Vice President chairs a new EOP-housed Task Force with broad authority to 'coordinate and accelerate' fraud-prevention policy across at least 12 federal agencies, including authority to recommend 'proactively pausing certain types of funding' (Sec 3(a)(ii)) based on the Task Force's own fraud-risk assessments.

The Task Force 'shall be subject to the President's direct supervision and control' (Sec 3(c)) with reporting only to the President rather than any public or Congressional reporting requirement specified in the order.

Bundles eligibility-verification policy development (Sec 3(a)(i)), pre-disbursement fraud controls including funding-pause authority (Sec 3(a)(ii)), interagency data-sharing (Sec 3(a)(iv)), network disruption/investigation coordination (Sec 3(a)(v)-(vi)), remittance-transfer restrictions (Sec 3(a)(vii)), state/local funding-conditionality development (Sec 4(b)), and private qui tam litigation promotion (Sec 6) across 12+ agencies in one order.

The criteria for 'proactively pausing certain types of funding' (Sec 3(a)(ii)) pending fraud-control establishment are undefined, and the order's Sec 1 narrative explicitly ties funding/enforcement posture to states' perceived political motivations ('self-dealing political actors,' 'sanctuary from Federal immigration laws') rather than a neutral fraud-risk standard alone.

Sec 4(b)'s directive to 'examine and recommend... ways that Federal funds may be withheld from jurisdictions that do not have adequate anti-fraud requirements,' combined with the order's Sec 1 framing that ties fraud tolerance to specific states' immigration and sanctuary policies, creates incentive for the funding-conditionality mechanism to be applied in a manner correlated with a state's political alignment rather than solely its objective fraud-control adequacy.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing Task Force.

Sec 3(c)'s provision that the Task Force operates under 'the President's direct supervision and control' rather than through normal agency-head accountability channels, combined with the funding-pause authority (Sec 3(a)(ii)) that could functionally interrupt Congressionally-appropriated benefit payments based on the Task Force's own internal risk assessment rather than a judicial or independent administrative process.

Sec 6's directive to 'promote the meritorious pursuit by private persons of civil actions' under the False Claims Act (31 U.S.C. 3730) depends on private whistleblowers' and qui tam relators' voluntary litigation decisions to supplement the Task Force's own enforcement capacity.

The funding-pause authority (Sec 3(a)(ii)) and funding-conditionality-for-states mechanism (Sec 4(b)), if applied broadly or in a manner correlated with a jurisdiction's political alignment as the order's Sec 1 narrative suggests, risks disrupting benefit delivery to eligible recipients in affected jurisdictions while fraud-control adequacy determinations are pending, a collateral effect on lawful beneficiaries that the order's fraud-prevention framing does not address.

At least 12 named agencies (Sec 2(c)(i)-(xii)) are all designated Task Force members with overlapping benefit-program responsibilities, coordinated only through the Task Force's own internal Chairman/Vice Chairman structure.
No distinct new exemption/waiver mechanism identified; this order is oriented toward tightening rather than creating carve-outs.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=10 (F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=1 (F6). raw=F1(1)+F2(1)+F3(1)+F4(1)+F5(1)+F7(1)+F8(1)+F9(1)+F10(1)+F11(0)=9. max=20. pct=9/20=45.0%. Notable: Sec 1's narrative explicitly ties funding posture to states' political alignment (immigration/sanctuary policy), superficially resembling the unilateral-federalism-preemption pattern; but structurally distinct -- this order conditions funding on program-integrity/anti-fraud administration rather than on states declining to enforce their own validly enacted law, a more conventional (if partisan-inflected) exercise of the spending power.

title: Preserving America's Game

flags.justification: Narrow, largely aspirational broadcast-scheduling coordination directive, not unusually concentrated.
Transparent, narrowly stated purpose and mechanism.
Single-subject, minimal-content order (broadcast-window coordination for one annual event).
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism; this is a voluntary-coordination request to private broadcast/media partners.
No incentive structure at issue.
No sunset structure at issue; ongoing annual-event policy.

Sec 2(b) directs the FCC Chairman to 'consider reviewing the public interest obligations of broadcast licensees' to potentially mandate protected broadcast status for a specific college football game, using FCC public-interest licensing authority (ordinarily focused on matters like local news, children's programming, or political-broadcast access) as a lever for a scheduling preference for a specific sporting event.

The entire practical effectiveness of the order depends on voluntary coordination and agreement by the CFP Committee, NCAA, and private broadcast/media rights partners, none of whom the order can compel (Sec 2(a): 'with the goal of establishing').
No notable spillover risk identified for a narrow scheduling-coordination request.
Commerce/FCC roles are clearly delineated.
No exemption/waiver architecture at issue.

title: Further Continuance of the Federal Emergency Management Agency Review Council

flags.justification: Routine, ministerial FACA-committee continuance, tied to a report deadline.
Fully FACA-governed, transparent, defined termination trigger (report submission or May 29, 2026, whichever first).
Single-subject, minimal-content order.
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism.
No incentive structure at issue.
Sec 1 ties termination to '10 days after the report... is submitted... or May 29, 2026, whichever occurs first' -- a well-defined, dual-trigger sunset.
Operates entirely within FACA's own renewal framework, now for a third consecutive continuance of the same council.
No third-party dependency at issue.
No notable spillover risk identified for a routine, report-tied committee-continuance order.
Secretary of Homeland Security is sole implementing official (Sec 2).
No exemption/waiver architecture at issue.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=6 (F1,F2,F6,F7,F9,F10), NA=5 (F3,F4,F5,F8,F11). raw=F1(0)+F2(0)+F6(1)+F7(0)+F9(0)+F10(0)=1. max=12. pct=1/12=8.33%. Third consecutive continuance of the FEMA Review Council (EO14180[8.33%]->EO14378[8.33%, Chunk10]->EO14397[this record]), all three identical -- confirms this stream's methodology treats the routine FACA-continuance mechanism consistently regardless of recurrence.

title: Addressing DEI Discrimination by Federal Contractors

flags.justification: OMB Director given authority to issue guidance ensuring compliance and to identify 'economic sectors that pose a particular risk' (Sec 4(b)), while contracting agencies are directed to cancel/terminate/debar based on a broadly defined 'racially discriminatory DEI activities' standard.

Agency heads 'review... and report' on compliance (Sec 4(c)) internally to the Assistant to the President for Domestic Policy rather than through any public reporting mechanism, and the 'economic sectors... risk' identification (Sec 4(b)) has no defined public criteria.

Bundles a mandatory contract clause requirement (Sec 3), agency penalty/debarment authority (Sec 4(a)), sector-risk identification (Sec 4(b)), FCA-liability designation (Sec 3, clause para 6), and FAR amendment directives (Sec 5) in one order.

'Racially discriminatory DEI activities' (Sec 2(a)) is defined broadly as 'disparate treatment... in the recruitment, employment... contracting... program participation, or allocation or deployment of an entity's resources,' broad enough to potentially capture a wide range of ordinary diversity-related business practices, determined by contracting officers with no independent adjudicative process specified before contract cancellation/suspension.

Designating compliance with the anti-DEI clause as 'material to the Government's payment decisions for purposes of section 3729(b)(4)... (False Claims Act)' (Sec 3, clause para 6) converts every Federal contract's DEI-compliance representation into a potential FCA liability trigger, creating a powerful incentive for contractors to over-comply (eliminating even lawful diversity programs) to avoid catastrophic treble-damages exposure, while Sec 4(d)(ii) simultaneously directs prompt review of private qui tam suits under this same theory -- creating a strong private bounty-hunting incentive around a broadly and ambiguously defined standard.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing contracting-policy requirement.

Directing agencies to cancel/terminate/suspend contracts and debar contractors for clause violations (Sec 4(a)) based on the contracting agency's own determination, without a specified pre-termination adjudicative process comparable to ordinary debarment procedures' due-process protections.
Sec 4(d)(ii)'s prioritization of private qui tam actions under the False Claims Act depends on private relators' voluntary litigation decisions to identify and pursue alleged violations.

Combining a broad, undefined 'racially discriminatory DEI activities' standard with FCA treble-damages exposure and debarment risk creates strong incentives for federal contractors to eliminate even lawful, non-discriminatory diversity-related programs and business practices out of legal caution, a chilling effect extending well beyond the order's stated target of genuinely discriminatory conduct.
OMB, DOJ, EEOC, and individual contracting agencies have reasonably delineated roles (OMB issues guidance, DOJ pursues FCA actions, contracting agencies enforce the clause).
No distinct new exemption/waiver mechanism identified; the order is enforcement-oriented rather than exemption-oriented.

title: Ensuring Citizenship Verification and Integrity in Federal Elections

flags.justification: DHS/SSA compile and transmit 'State Citizenship Lists' to all state election officials (Sec 2(a)), and the Postmaster General is directed to promulgate a national rulemaking establishing uniform ballot-tracking/enrollment requirements for all state mail-in/absentee ballot systems (Sec 3) -- a sweeping federal role in an area the Constitution primarily assigns to states.

The order itself acknowledges 'State and Federal laws and State procedures must still be followed' (Sec 2(a)) and that 'there may be State laws... that preclude voter registration,' yet proceeds with a nationwide data-compilation and USPS-rulemaking apparatus without a defined mechanism for resolving conflicts between the federal lists/procedures and state-specific election law.

Bundles a nationwide State Citizenship List compilation/transmission system (Sec 2(a)), DOJ investigation/prosecution prioritization directives (Sec 2(b)), a USPS ballot-tracking rulemaking with State enrollment-list requirements (Sec 3), and a funding-withholding enforcement mechanism (Sec 5) across DHS, SSA, DOJ, USPS, and Commerce in one order.

Sec 5's enforcement directive to withhold 'Federal funds from noncompliant States and localities where such withholding is authorized by law' does not specify which programs' funds are at issue or a defined compliance-determination process before withholding.
No notable incentive misalignment identified beyond the mechanisms already flagged; the underlying citizenship-verification goal aligns with stated federal election-integrity law.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing citizenship-verification and mail-ballot infrastructure.

Sec 3's USPS rulemaking would establish, via a single federal agency's rulemaking, a national framework requiring States to affirmatively 'notify' USPS and submit voter-specific enrollment lists before USPS will transmit any state's mail-in or absentee ballots at all (Sec 3(b)(iii): 'USPS shall not transmit mail-in or absentee ballots from any individual unless' enrolled) -- a mechanism that could functionally condition a state's entire vote-by-mail system's postal viability on compliance with a federally designed enrollment/tracking process, in an area of election administration the Elections Clause assigns primarily to state legislatures subject to Congressional (not executive-agency) regulation.

The entire State Citizenship List and Mail-In/Absentee Participation List system depends on states' voluntary cooperation in supplementing, correcting, and using the federally compiled lists (Sec 2(a), Sec 3(b)(ii)-(v)); states are not compelled to adopt vote-by-mail through USPS at all, though for those that do the order's mechanism becomes effectively mandatory.

Establishing a nationwide, DHS/SSA-compiled citizen-status list transmitted to all state election officials, combined with a USPS rulemaking that could condition mail-ballot transmission on state-specific voter enrollment/tracking systems, creates significant implementation and error-rate risk (citizenship records can be incomplete or outdated, particularly for naturalized citizens or those with name changes) with the potential to disenfranchise eligible voters if list errors are not promptly correctable; combined with Sec 5's funding-withholding threat against 'noncompliant States,' this creates unprecedented federal leverage over state election administration processes constitutionally reserved substantially to the states.

DHS, SSA, USPS, DOJ, and Commerce are all independently tasked with overlapping roles in the citizenship-verification and ballot-integrity system without one single clearly accountable coordinating lead beyond general 'coordination' language (Sec 4(a)).
No distinct new exemption/waiver mechanism identified; this order is a verification/enforcement mechanism rather than an exemption architecture.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=10 (F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=1 (F6). raw=F1(1)+F2(1)+F3(1)+F4(1)+F5(0)+F7(2)+F8(1)+F9(2)+F10(1)+F11(0)=10. max=20. pct=10/20=50.0%. Notable finding: a novel federal-election-administration preemption mechanism operating through USPS rulemaking rather than funding conditionality or litigation -- structurally distinct from this stream's other unilateral-federalism-preemption instances -- see pattern log.

title: Urgent National Action To Save College Sports

flags.justification: Agency heads across the federal contracting/grantmaking apparatus are directed to evaluate violations of a PRIVATE governing body's rules as grounds for federal suspension/debarment determinations against universities (Sec 4(a)), effectively delegating a quasi-regulatory role to a private association's rulebook.

'Cause so serious or compelling in nature to affect the present responsibility of the recipient' (Sec 4(a)(i)) is a broad, discretionary standard for triggering suspension/debarment based on a private association's rule violations, without a defined independent review process.

Bundles federal contractor/grantee suspension-debarment leverage tied to private-association-rule compliance (Sec 4(a)), detailed substantive prescriptions for what those private rules should contain (eligibility limits, transfer rules, revenue-sharing, medical care, NIL prohibitions, agent registry) (Sec 4(b)), FTC enforcement against student-athlete agents (Sec 4(e)), and DOJ litigation directives to invalidate STATE LAWS that conflict with the private governing body's rules (Sec 5) -- an extraordinarily wide-ranging intervention into nominally private/state-regulated activity, using federal contracting leverage as the enforcement mechanism.

'Improper financial activities' and 'fraudulent NIL scheme' (Sec 3(a)-(b)) involve fact-intensive, multi-factor definitions (e.g., 'above the actual fair market value') that will require substantial case-by-case interpretation, with federal contracting consequences potentially riding on that interpretation.

Conditioning universities' federal research/grant relationships (defense, HHS, NSF funding, per Sec 1) on compliance with a private association's athletics rules creates pressure for the private governing body's rulemaking process to align with federal preferences to avoid triggering funding consequences for its member institutions, blurring the line between private self-governance and federally-directed policy.
No sunset structure at issue; the order establishes an ongoing compliance framework effective August 1, 2026.

Sec 5 directs the Attorney General to 'further meritorious actions to invalidate State laws that conflict with interstate intercollegiate athletic governing body rules,' explicitly deploying DOJ litigation resources to preempt state legislation (the order's own Sec 1 acknowledges such state laws were enacted by 'State legislatures... incentivized to advantage their own State's universities') via Commerce Clause and Contracts Clause theories -- continuing this stream's unilateral-federalism-preemption pattern, but here directed at protecting a PRIVATE association's rules from state legislative override rather than a federal regulatory scheme.

The entire framework depends on the 'interstate intercollegiate athletic governing body' voluntarily adopting the detailed rule prescriptions in Sec 4(b) 'in its discretion' -- the order cannot directly mandate a private association's rules, only incentivize adoption via the federal contracting leverage in Sec 4(a).

Using federal research/grant contracting leverage to enforce compliance with a private association's rules, combined with DOJ litigation to invalidate state laws that conflict with those same private rules, is a structurally novel two-pronged enforcement approach directed at protecting private self-regulation from both state legislative override and internal non-compliance -- with unclear boundaries on how the association's rules, which could change over time at its own discretion, will continue to satisfy the order's substantive prescriptions (Sec 4(b)) as a moving target for federal contracting consequences.

The Department of War, HHS, NSF, and all other federal contracting/grantmaking agencies are independently directed to evaluate the same private association's rule compliance (Sec 4(a)) without a single coordinating lead beyond general OMB guidance.
No distinct new exemption/waiver mechanism identified beyond Sec 4(b)(i)(A)'s exceptions for 'military service, missionary service, and other periods of absence.'

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=10 (F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=1 (F6). raw=F1(1)+F2(1)+F3(2)+F4(1)+F5(1)+F7(2)+F8(1)+F9(1)+F10(1)+F11(0)=11. max=20. pct=11/20=55.0%. Second-generation order in the college-sports lineage begun by EO14322 [Chunk8, this stream:50.0%]. Notable finding: uses federal contracting leverage to enforce compliance with a PRIVATE association's rules while simultaneously directing DOJ litigation to preempt state laws that conflict with those same private rules -- a novel 'private-rule-protection' variant of this stream's unilateral-federalism-preemption pattern.

title: Accelerating Medical Treatments for Serious Mental Illness

flags.justification: Directs FDA/DEA/AG to use existing statutory tools (priority vouchers, Right to Try Act, CSA rescheduling review), not unusually concentrated.
The order operates through defined, existing statutory processes (Breakthrough Therapy designation, Right to Try Act, CSA Section 811 rescheduling) with their own established criteria.

Bundles FDA priority-voucher allocation (Sec 2(a)), Right to Try/Schedule I handling authorization (Sec 2(b)), HHS-State collaborative funding (Sec 3), VA/private-sector data-sharing (Sec 4), and CSA rescheduling review (Sec 5) across five distinct mechanisms in one order.
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism; this order concerns drug-access/approval acceleration.

No notable incentive misalignment identified; using existing accelerated-approval and Right to Try mechanisms for drugs with Breakthrough Therapy designation is a conventional (if optimistic) approach.
No sunset structure at issue for ongoing drug-development-acceleration mechanisms.
Directs use of the FDA's own statutory tools (National Priority Voucher Program, Right to Try Act, Breakthrough Therapy designation) rather than bypassing an established check.
Sec 3's HHS-State collaborative funding depends on States having 'enacted or are developing programs to advance psychedelic drugs,' a voluntary state-level dependency.

Accelerating access to Schedule I psychedelic compounds (including ibogaine, which carries documented cardiac-safety risks) via Right to Try pathways before full FDA approval, for a patient population with 'serious mental illness' who may face heightened vulnerability, creates safety-monitoring challenges not fully addressed by the order's general data-sharing and collaboration directives.
FDA, DEA, HHS, and VA roles are reasonably delineated by function (approval, scheduling, funding, veteran-specific coordination).

Sec 2(b) directs establishing 'necessary Schedule I handling authorizations' as a pathway for treating physicians/researchers to access controlled substances outside the normal DEA registration process, functioning as a targeted exemption mechanism.

title: Promoting Efficiency, Accountability, and Performance in Federal Contracting

flags.justification: Directs a procurement-policy default (fixed-price contracting) with defined, graduated approval thresholds by agency (Sec 2(b)(ii)), a reasonably structured delegation.
The order requires written justification for exceptions (Sec 2(b)(i)) and semi-annual reporting to OMB (Sec 2(d)), providing genuine transparency/accountability mechanisms.
Single-subject order (fixed-price contracting default policy), applied consistently across a coherent procurement-reform theme.
This order addresses internal federal procurement policy rather than enforcement against private parties.

Defaulting to fixed-price contracting for inherently uncertain-scope work (while excluding only R&D/pre-production development, Sec 2(b)(iv)) could incentivize contractors to underbid on complex projects with unclear requirements, then seek costly change orders or cut corners on quality/scope to preserve margins under a fixed price -- a classic fixed-price-contracting risk not addressed by the order's exceptions.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing procurement-policy default.
Operates through the existing FAR framework and standard agency-head approval-delegation processes rather than bypassing an established check.
Direct internal procurement-policy mechanism; no significant third-party voluntary-compliance dependency for the core default rule.

Shifting the presumptive contract type government-wide without a corresponding acknowledgment of the significant contracting-workforce training and cost-estimation capability needed to properly price fixed-price contracts for complex/novel requirements (addressed only via Sec 3(b)(ii)'s later-developed training program) risks near-term implementation friction or degraded contract outcomes during the transition period.
OMB/Administrator for Federal Procurement Policy provide clear central coordination (Sec 3) with individual agency heads retaining defined, graduated approval authority.

Sec 2(b)(iv) creates defined exceptions for emergency/contingency-operation contracts and R&D/pre-production development contracts, and Sec 2(b)(ii)-(iii) creates a graduated, agency-specific dollar-threshold exemption structure for non-fixed-price contract approval.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=9 (F1,F2,F3,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=2 (F4,F6). raw=F1(0)+F2(0)+F3(0)+F5(1)+F7(0)+F8(0)+F9(1)+F10(0)+F11(1)=3. max=18. pct=3/18=16.67%. A relatively well-structured, low-scoring procurement-reform order -- genuine transparency mechanisms (written justification, semi-annual OMB reporting) and defined graduated thresholds keep this order's score low despite touching all federal contracting.

title: Promoting Retirement-Savings Access for American Workers by Establishing TrumpIRA.gov

flags.justification: Directs Treasury to build an informational website with defined objective criteria (Sec 2(c)), not unusually concentrated.
The qualifying criteria for listed IRAs are objectively defined (cost caps, investment-option types, no minimum-balance requirements, Sec 2(c)), a transparent standard.

Bundles website/informational-platform establishment (Sec 2), Federal Saver's Match implementation support (Sec 3), tax-treatment guidance for charitable contributions (Sec 4), worker-protection regulatory directives (Sec 5), and a legislative-recommendation directive (Sec 6) in one order.
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism; this is an informational/facilitative platform.
No notable incentive misalignment identified; the objective cost/quality criteria for platform-listed IRAs (Sec 2(c)) are reasonably designed to avoid adverse selection.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing informational platform.
Implements the existing statutory Federal Saver's Match program (SECURE 2.0 Act) rather than bypassing an established check.

The platform's usefulness depends entirely on private financial institutions voluntarily offering IRAs meeting the listed cost/quality criteria and choosing to participate in the Federal Saver's Match program (Sec 3(b): 'encourage financial institutions to accept').
No notable spillover risk identified; this is a facilitative/informational mechanism built around an existing bipartisan statutory program (SECURE 2.0 Act) with objective listing criteria.
Treasury is clear lead with Labor/IRS in defined supporting roles.
No exemption/waiver architecture at issue.

title: Imposing Sanctions on Those Responsible for Repression in Cuba and for Threats to United States National Security and Foreign Policy

flags.justification: Secretary of State/Treasury given broad, largely unreviewable discretion to designate 'any foreign person' meeting an extensive list of criteria (Sec 2(a)(i)(A)-(I)) for asset blocking, and Sec 2(e) explicitly eliminates any prior-notice requirement before such designations take effect.

Designation criteria include broad catch-alls such as operating in 'any other sector of the Cuban economy, as may be determined by the Secretary of the Treasury' (Sec 2(a)(i)(A)) and extending liability to 'adult family member[s]' of designated persons (Sec 2(a)(i)(I)) without independent review.
Bundles asset-blocking sanctions (Sec 2), a travel/entry suspension (Sec 3), and secondary sanctions against foreign financial institutions (Sec 4) in one order.

Several designation criteria (e.g., 'to have materially assisted, sponsored, or provided financial, material, or technological support,' Sec 2(a)(i)(D)) are broad and could sweep in ordinary commercial activity with Cuba absent further specification.
No notable incentive misalignment identified beyond the standard sanctions-implementation dynamic.
No sunset/expiration mechanism; sanctions persist until affirmatively delisted.

Sec 2(e)'s explicit elimination of prior notice before a designation takes effect (justified by asset-flight risk) forecloses any pre-deprivation process for affected persons with a 'constitutional presence in the United States,' relying entirely on post-hoc administrative/judicial review avenues.
Direct executive designation/blocking mechanism; no significant third-party voluntary-compliance dependency.

The 'family member' designation extension (Sec 2(a)(i)(I)) and broad 'any other sector' catch-all (Sec 2(a)(i)(A)) create significant collateral-designation risk for individuals with only attenuated connections to the sanctionable conduct, an effect the order's general licensing mechanism only partially mitigates.
State/Treasury share a clearly delineated, standard sanctions-implementation structure.

Sec 2(b) preserves licenses issued under the pre-existing Cuban Assets Control Regulations (31 CFR part 515), and the order's own licensing mechanism (Sec 2(b)) creates the standard sanctions carve-out architecture.

title: Integrating Financial Technology Innovation into Regulatory Frameworks

flags.justification: Directs Federal financial regulators to conduct reviews with defined scope, and notably uses 'is requested' language for the independent Federal Reserve (Sec 4) rather than directive language, reflecting institutional respect for Fed independence.
The order requires the Federal Reserve to submit a public report with findings/options/recommendations (Sec 4(b)) within a defined 120-day window, a genuine transparency mechanism.

Bundles a general regulatory-barrier review across six federal financial regulators (Sec 3), a detailed Federal Reserve payment-account-access evaluation (Sec 4), and access-procedure establishment directives (Sec 4(c)) in one order.
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism; this order addresses regulatory review and access-policy evaluation.
No notable incentive misalignment identified.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing regulatory-review framework.

The order's careful use of 'requested' rather than 'directed' language for the Federal Reserve (Sec 4(a)-(c)) reflects deference to, rather than a bypass of, the Fed's statutory independence -- a notable contrast to EO14393's (Chunk10, this stream: 55.56%) more assertive 'shall consider' framing directed at the same category of independent regulators.

Sec 3(a)'s review of barriers to 'partnerships with federally regulated institutions' depends on fintech firms' and banks' voluntary interest in forming such partnerships once barriers are identified/reduced.

Expanding direct Reserve Bank payment-account access to 'covered firms' including non-bank digital-asset companies (Sec 4(b)) could, if implemented, create novel financial-stability and payment-system-integrity questions given these firms' more limited prudential regulation compared to traditional banks -- a risk the order's own 'appropriate risk management requirements' language (Sec 4(b)(ii)) acknowledges only in passing.
Six named Federal financial regulators each retain their own defined review scope (Sec 3), with the Federal Reserve's payment-access evaluation kept analytically separate (Sec 4).
No exemption/waiver architecture at issue; this order is review/evaluation-oriented.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=8 (F1,F2,F3,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10), NA=3 (F4,F6,F11). raw=F1(0)+F2(0)+F3(1)+F5(0)+F7(0)+F8(1)+F9(1)+F10(0)=3. max=16. pct=3/16=18.75%. Diff note: notably deferential 'requested' language for Fed actions (Sec 4), contrasting with EO14393's [Chunk10, this stream:55.56%] more assertive 'shall consider' framing for the same category of independent regulators -- a rare instance of measurable restraint toward agency independence within this batch.

title: Restoring Integrity to America's Financial System

flags.justification: Directs Treasury/financial regulators to issue advisories/guidance through existing BSA/CFPB regulatory tools, not unusually concentrated in a single office.

The Advisory's 'red flags and typologies' (Sec 3(a)) link routine ITIN usage, cash-based wage payment patterns common among many small businesses/informal workers generally, and other broad indicators to a 'non-work authorized population' risk category, without a defined process for financial institutions to distinguish false positives from actual violations before triggering enhanced due diligence.

Bundles a formal Treasury Advisory on immigration-status-linked financial-crime typologies (Sec 3(a)), BSA customer-due-diligence rule changes (Sec 3(b)-(c)), CFPB ability-to-repay guidance tying immigration status to creditworthiness (Sec 4(a)), and financial-regulator credit-risk guidance (Sec 4(b)) across at least five agencies in one order.

Sec 3(a)(vi) directs treating ITIN usage 'in lieu of a Social Security number or valid work-authorized visa' as a 'risk factor requiring enhanced due diligence,' without a defined threshold distinguishing ordinary, lawful ITIN use (common among lawfully present non-citizens, resident aliens for tax purposes, and others) from actual illicit activity.

Directing the CFPB to consider clarifying that 'potential deportation and loss of wages' may be treated as an ability-to-repay risk factor (Sec 4(a)) could incentivize lenders to systematically deny credit based on perceived immigration status rather than individualized creditworthiness, potentially sweeping in lawfully present individuals whose status is misjudged, while also creating disparate-impact risk under fair-lending laws not addressed in the order.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing regulatory-guidance framework.

Directing financial institutions to consider immigration-status indicators (ITIN usage, wage-payment patterns) as BSA/AML risk factors and credit-underwriting factors effectively deputizes banks as an extension of immigration enforcement, layering immigration-status verification onto financial-services access in a manner not contemplated by the BSA's core anti-money-laundering/counter-terrorism-financing purpose.

The entire framework depends on financial institutions voluntarily incorporating the Advisory's typologies into their own risk-based compliance programs (Sec 3(b)-(c) direct only 'proposed changes' to regulations, not immediate binding requirements).

Linking routine financial-services indicators (ITIN use, cash-wage patterns, informal payroll practices common among small businesses broadly, not just those employing unauthorized workers) to enhanced due diligence and credit-risk flagging creates meaningful risk of financial exclusion for lawfully present immigrants, mixed-status families, and cash-intensive small businesses generally, an effect not analyzed against the order's stated national-security/fraud-prevention purpose.
Treasury is clear lead with CFPB and the 'Federal functional financial regulators' in defined, complementary supporting roles.
No exemption/waiver architecture at issue; this order is a risk-flagging/guidance mechanism.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=9 (F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10), NA=2 (F6,F11). raw=F1(0)+F2(1)+F3(1)+F4(1)+F5(1)+F7(1)+F8(1)+F9(1)+F10(0)=7. max=18. pct=7/18=38.89%. Notable finding: financial-institution risk-flagging based on immigration-status proxies (ITIN usage, cash-wage patterns) -- a novel mechanism blending BSA/AML compliance with immigration enforcement, distinct from this stream's other immigration-related findings which typically involved direct agency action rather than privatized financial-sector deputization.

title: Realigning United States Core Childhood Vaccine Recommendations with Best Practices from Peer, Developed Countries

flags.justification: Directs CDC/ACIP to conduct their own scientific review process using existing statutory advisory-committee mechanisms, not unusually concentrated.

Relies on ACIP's established, semi-independent advisory-committee review process (though implemented pursuant to a Presidential Memorandum's 'scientific assessment' as a 'guiding resource,' Sec 2(a)) rather than a purely internal executive determination.

Single-subject order (childhood vaccine schedule alignment), though touching insurance-coverage mandates and state-notification requirements as implementation details rather than distinct bundled subjects.
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism; this order addresses a public-health advisory/coverage-policy process.

No notable incentive misalignment identified; the order explicitly preserves coverage without cost-sharing for whatever schedule ACIP ultimately adopts (Sec 2(c)), avoiding a coverage-reduction incentive.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing vaccine-schedule review process.

Directing CDC/ACIP to review a scientific assessment produced pursuant to a Presidential Memorandum (rather than ACIP's own independently-initiated review process) and to 'take any appropriate steps to update' the schedule based on that assessment (Sec 2(b)) inserts a degree of executive-branch policy direction into what is traditionally an ACIP-driven, expert-committee-led scientific process, even though the order stops short of mandating specific outcomes.
Direct HHS/CDC/ACIP regulatory-review mechanism; no significant third-party voluntary-compliance dependency.

Potentially reducing the recommended core childhood vaccine schedule to more closely match 'peer, developed countries' (Sec 1) could have significant public-health effects on population immunity depending on the specific vaccines affected, an outcome contingent entirely on ACIP's yet-to-be-completed review (Sec 2(b)) and therefore not fully assessable from this order's text alone, though the order's preservation of coverage/access (Sec 2(c)) mitigates some access-related risk.
CDC/ACIP is the clear lead with HHS's Office of Intergovernmental and External Affairs in a defined state-notification support role.
No exemption/waiver architecture at issue.

title: Removing Unnecessary and Counterproductive Restrictions on Access to Federal Lands

flags.justification: A rescission order removing specific-criteria requirements rather than concentrating new authority; land-management decisions revert to agencies' existing statutory authorities.

Rescinding the 'minimize harassment of wildlife,' 'minimize conflicts,' and 'not adversely affect natural, aesthetic, or scenic values' criteria (Sec 1) without a defined replacement standard removes specific, articulated public-interest criteria that agencies were required to apply, relying instead on the 'appropriate framework' of general statutory authorities without specifying what that framework requires going forward.
Single-subject order (rescission of two related off-road-vehicle-designation orders).
This order rescinds prior direction rather than establishing an enforcement standard.
No notable incentive misalignment identified.
No sunset structure at issue; this is a permanent rescission.
The underlying statutory frameworks (NEPA, ESA, NHPA, FLPMA) remain in place per the order's own Sec 1 acknowledgment; no distinct bypass of an established check beyond the rescission itself.
No third-party dependency at issue.

Rescinding two 50-year-old executive orders that provided specific, articulated wildlife/conflict/scenic-value criteria for off-road-vehicle designation decisions, and directing agencies to 'initiate rulemakings to rescind or revise' their implementing regulations (Sec 2), removes a decades-old, judicially-referenced framework for balancing off-road-vehicle access against environmental and recreational-conflict concerns without specifying what standard will govern in the interim rulemaking period, creating a potential regulatory gap during the transition and shifting substantial discretion to individual land-management agencies' general statutory authorities with less specific guidance than the rescinded orders provided.

Secretary of War, Secretary of the Interior, Secretary of Agriculture, and TVA are each independently directed to rescind/revise their own implementing regulations, a reasonably parallel (if uncoordinated) structure.
No exemption/waiver architecture at issue; this is a rescission rather than a carve-out mechanism.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=6 (F1,F2,F5,F7,F9,F10), NA=5 (F3,F4,F6,F8,F11). raw=F1(0)+F2(1)+F5(0)+F7(0)+F9(2)+F10(0)=3. max=12. pct=3/12=25.0%. Rescinds EO11644 [this stream:16.67%] and EO11989 [this stream:12.5%] with no replacement framework -- a wholesale-revocation pattern structurally similar to EO14337 [Chunk8, this stream:20.0%, CRITICAL F9 only], again producing a low applicable-flag count precisely because the order itself does little beyond the rescission, while its second-order effect (regulatory-gap risk) is significant.

title: Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security

flags.justification: The Director of NSA (in consultation with others) is given authority to determine the classified benchmarking threshold for designating an AI model a 'covered frontier model' (Sec 3(a)), a consequential technical/regulatory-adjacent determination made through a classified process not subject to public review.

The 'covered frontier model' benchmarking process and threshold determination (Sec 3(a)) is explicitly classified, precluding public or even most Congressional visibility into how or why a given AI model would be designated as posing advanced cyber capabilities.

Bundles Federal information-system cyber-defense prioritization (Sec 2), a novel frontier-AI-model early-access/review framework (Sec 3), AI-enabled-crime enforcement prioritization (Sec 4), and cross-agency cybersecurity clearinghouse creation (Sec 2(d)) across DOD/War, DHS, Treasury, NSA, and OMB in one order.

Sec 4's directive to prioritize prosecution under existing computer-crime statutes (18 U.S.C. 1028/1030/1343) applies established, judicially-tested statutory standards rather than creating a new vague enforcement standard.
No notable incentive misalignment identified; the 'voluntary framework' language (Sec 3(b)) for frontier-model engagement avoids compelling participation.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing AI-security coordination framework.

Establishing a classified process (Sec 3(a)) by which the NSA Director determines whether private AI models must be shared with the federal government 'for a period of up to 30 days before they plan to release such models' (Sec 3(b)(ii)) creates a de facto pre-release government review mechanism for advanced AI models, even though Sec 3(c) explicitly and unusually disclaims any 'mandatory governmental licensing, preclearance, or permitting requirement' -- a notable self-limiting provision, though the practical distinction between a 'voluntary' 30-day pre-release access framework tied to classified national-security benchmarking and a mandatory preclearance regime may be narrower in practice than the order's text suggests.

The entire frontier-model framework (Sec 3(b)) depends on AI developers' voluntary participation in engaging with the government to determine 'covered frontier model' status and provide pre-release access, with no compulsion mechanism given Sec 3(c)'s explicit disclaimer.

Creating a classified benchmarking process and voluntary pre-release access framework for the most advanced AI models establishes infrastructure that, while framed as voluntary and security-focused, could evolve into a more mandatory review regime in the future without requiring a new executive order, given the framework and relationships (trusted-partner selection, Sec 3(b)(iii)) would already be established.

The Committee on National Security Systems, Department of War, DHS/CISA, Treasury, NSA, and OMB are all independently tasked with overlapping cyber-defense and frontier-model-review responsibilities without one single clearly accountable coordinating lead beyond loose 'consultation' and 'coordination' language throughout.
No distinct new exemption/waiver mechanism identified; Sec 3(c)'s disclaimer of mandatory licensing is a limitation on the order's own scope rather than an exemption from an external requirement.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=9 (F1,F2,F3,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=2 (F4,F6). raw=F1(1)+F2(1)+F3(1)+F5(0)+F7(1)+F8(1)+F9(1)+F10(1)+F11(0)=7. max=18. pct=7/18=38.89%. Notable: Sec 3(c)'s explicit disclaimer of mandatory licensing/preclearance is a rare self-limiting provision within this batch, though the practical distinction from the classified benchmarking/30-day pre-release framework it sits alongside may be narrower than the disclaimer suggests.

title: Implementing Schedule Policy/Career in the Excepted Service

flags.justification: Transfers a defined (Appendix-listed) set of federal positions out of competitive-service adverse-action protections into Schedule Policy/Career, continuing and operationalizing the mechanism this stream has previously scored as the single highest-scoring order in the entire corpus (EO14171, 83.33%).

The 'confidential, policy-determining, policy-making, or policy-advocating character' standard (Sec 5(a)) for determining which positions qualify for Schedule Policy/Career transfer is applied by agency heads without a defined independent review, and Sec 3(a)(ii)'s amendment broadens the agency-head petition process for future placements.

Bundles Civil Service Rule amendments (Sec 2), amendments to two prior Executive Orders (EO13957/14171 and EO13562/14217) (Sec 3), a new performance-award/bonus-pool mechanism specific to Schedule Policy/Career employees (Sec 4), and the actual position-by-position transfer via Appendix (Sec 5) in one order.

New 5 CFR 550.704(b)(6) (Sec 2(f)) permits severance-pay denial whenever an agency 'identifies unacceptable performance or misconduct as the basis for separation in a written notice,' a standard determined entirely by the agency itself with the severance-pay consequence attached.

Removing standard adverse-action procedural protections for policy-influencing positions (continuing the Schedule F/Policy-Career mechanism this stream has repeatedly flagged) while simultaneously creating a new performance-bonus pool specifically for the same employee category (Sec 4) creates a combined carrot-and-stick structure that could incentivize policy-influencing career employees to align actions with political leadership's preferences to secure both job security and bonus eligibility, rather than exercising independent professional judgment.
No sunset structure at issue; the position transfers and rule amendments are permanent absent future rescission.

Continuing the mechanism this stream has repeatedly analyzed (EO13957/EO14171), the position-transfer process removes standard competitive-service adverse-action procedural protections (formal charges, opportunity to respond, MSPB appeal rights) for a class of career employees, now with actual position-by-position implementation via this order's Appendix.
No third-party dependency at issue; this is a direct internal personnel-policy mechanism.

Actually transferring specific, named positions into Schedule Policy/Career (rather than merely establishing the schedule's existence, as EO14171 did) operationalizes the reduced job-security framework for a now-concrete set of career civil servants, with downstream effects on institutional knowledge retention and career-employee willingness to provide candid, non-political policy advice that the order's Sec 1 'accountability' framing does not address.
OPM is the clear central coordinating authority (Sec 2(g), Sec 4(b)) with individual agency heads implementing position-specific transfers.

Sec 3(a)(ii)'s amendment continues the 'petition' process by which agency heads must seek OPM/Presidential approval to place additional positions in Schedule Policy/Career, and various Civil Service Rule amendments (Sec 2(d), 2(e)) create schedule-specific exemptions from standard trial-period and severance-qualification rules.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=9 (F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F7,F9,F10,F11), NA=2 (F6,F8). raw=F1(1)+F2(1)+F3(1)+F4(1)+F5(1)+F7(1)+F9(1)+F10(0)+F11(1)=8. max=18. pct=8/18=44.44%. Diff note: operationalizes the Schedule F/Policy-Career mechanism established by EO13957 [this stream:37.5%] and expanded by EO14171 [this stream:83.33%, the highest score in this stream's corpus] -- this order's lower score reflects that it is the position-by-position implementation step (narrower, more mechanical) rather than the foundational authority-creating order, even though it operationalizes the same underlying mechanism this stream has repeatedly flagged as high-severity.

title: Strengthening Customs Enforcement

flags.justification: Secretary of Homeland Security given broad discretionary authority across nearly every stage of customs enforcement (importer eligibility, 'good standing' definition, disclosure requirements, penalty mitigation standards, disposal procedures) via numerous 'as the Secretary deems necessary' delegations.

'Good standing' (Sec 2(d)) is left to CBP's own definition based on unspecified 'other relevant considerations,' and Sec 2(c)'s 'located in the United States' determination for IOR classification purposes is left to the Secretary's own guidance without defined objective criteria beyond three minimum factors.

Bundles importer-of-record eligibility/bonding reform (Sec 2), heightened disclosure/certification requirements (Sec 3), enforcement/penalty-mitigation-standard revisions including a new penalty floor (Sec 4), streamlined seizure/disposal procedures (Sec 5), transparency requirements (Sec 6), and a legislative-recommendation directive (Sec 8) in one order.

'Good standing' determinations (Sec 2(d)) and the discretionary 'other data that CBP deems necessary' disclosure requirement (Sec 2(a)(iii)) leave substantial undefined discretion in CBP's hands for determinations with significant import-eligibility consequences.
No notable incentive misalignment identified; establishing minimum penalty floors and eliminating mitigation for repeat offenders (Sec 4(c)) is a conventional deterrence-strengthening approach.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing customs-enforcement framework.

Sec 4(c)'s directive to establish 'a minimum penalty floor of not less than 50 percent of the assessed penalty, absent exceptional circumstances' and to 'eliminat[e] mitigation for repeat offenders' substantially constrains CBP's own case-by-case penalty-mitigation discretion that importers have historically relied on, functioning as a self-imposed floor that forecloses the agency's own normal individualized-assessment process.
Direct regulatory/enforcement mechanism; the order's core mechanisms do not depend on third-party voluntary action.

Explicitly differentiating 'foreign IOR' and 'U.S. IOR' treatment (prohibiting informal entry, requiring CTPAT validation, restricting continuous-bond use for foreign IORs specifically, Sec 2(b)-(c)) based on nationality/organizational-location criteria creates a two-tiered customs-compliance system whose consistency with international trade-law non-discrimination principles the order does not address, even as Sec 7's unusually detailed 'Consideration of Relevant Issues' section pre-emptively defends the policy judgment against administrative-law arbitrary-and-capricious challenges.
Secretary of Homeland Security (through CBP) is the clear lead with DOJ in a defined supporting enforcement role (Sec 4(b)).

Sec 2(b)(i) creates an exception allowing CBP to permit continuous-bond use for foreign IORs when revenue would be 'fully protected,' and the order's differential treatment of U.S. versus foreign IORs itself constitutes a structured, criteria-based exemption/heightened-requirement architecture.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=10 (F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=1 (F6). raw=F1(1)+F2(1)+F3(1)+F4(1)+F5(0)+F7(1)+F8(0)+F9(1)+F10(0)+F11(1)=7. max=20. pct=7/20=35.0%. Notable: Sec 7's unusually explicit 'Consideration of Relevant Issues' section functions as a pre-emptive administrative-law defense against arbitrary-and-capricious review -- a structurally novel drafting technique not previously seen in this corpus, suggesting increased legal sophistication/risk-awareness in later-batch order drafting.

title: Ushering in the Next Frontier of Quantum Innovation

flags.justification: The Assistant to the President for Science and Technology given broad coordinating authority across the National Quantum Strategy update, the QC-ADDS Effort, and numerous cross-agency workforce/supply-chain/international-engagement initiatives (Sec 3-9), consolidating a sweeping science-and-security agenda under White House Office coordination.

The order requires numerous defined reports to the President/OMB/APST on specific timelines (Sec 10), and directs public release of technical specifications 'as appropriate' (Sec 4(c)), providing at least partial transparency mechanisms typical of this order type.

Bundles a National Quantum Strategy update (Sec 3), a new national quantum-computer development effort with a DOE-facility delivery target (Sec 4), quantum-sensor/network deployment across four agencies with 5-year plans (Sec 5), domestic supply-chain strengthening (Sec 6), a counterintelligence/security framework including FBI staffing expansion (Sec 7), workforce development across OPM/Labor/NSF (Sec 8), and international-engagement coordination (Sec 9) -- seven major initiative areas spanning at least ten federal entities, the third instance this batch of a Manhattan-Project-scale bundling pattern (after EO14363 Genesis Mission and EO14369 Space Superiority).
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism; this is a research/coordination/security initiative.

Sec 6(b)'s directive to 'potentially us[e] prize challenges or advance market commitments' to develop quantum-enabling technologies, combined with Sec 4(d)'s directive to explore 'advance market commitments' for the QC-ADDS Effort itself, creates a mechanism where private companies' technology-development priorities could be substantially shaped by anticipated government purchase commitments rather than independent commercial viability assessments.
No sunset structure at issue for an ongoing, multi-year national strategy framework.

Sec 7(a)-(b) establishes a classified/security-focused 'robust and balanced security controls' framework and expands the Quantum Information Science and Technology Counterintelligence Protection Team's staffing and scope for 'coordinat[ing] public messaging and outreach' to industry and academia regarding security matters, functioning as a government-directed information/access-control layer over academic and private-sector quantum research without a defined public accountability mechanism for how 'balanced' the controls actually are in practice.

Substantial portions of the Strategy's success (the QC-ADDS Effort's delivery timeline, supply-chain strengthening, technology-transfer to trusted partners) depend on voluntary private-sector partnership models (Sec 4(d): 'explore potential private-sector partnership models') and prize-challenge/advance-market-commitment participation, none of which the order can compel.

Simultaneously pursuing a major new quantum-computing hardware-delivery effort (Sec 4), quantum-sensor fielding by a specific 2028 date (Sec 5(a)), domestic supply-chain reconstruction (Sec 6), expanded counterintelligence staffing (Sec 7(b)), and a government-wide QIST workforce strategy (Sec 8) -- each individually a multi-year, resource-intensive undertaking -- within a single order and largely overlapping implementation timeline creates substantial cross-agency resource-competition and coordination risk comparable to this stream's findings for EO14363 (Genesis Mission) and EO14369 (Space Superiority), without a single empowered coordinating body beyond the APST's general 'coordination' role across all seven initiative areas.

War, Commerce, Energy, DNI, NSF, NASA, FBI, NSA, Labor, and State are all independently tasked with substantial, overlapping responsibilities across the order's seven initiative areas without one single accountable operational lead beyond the APST's loose coordinating role.
No distinct new exemption/waiver mechanism identified; the order creates coordination/security-review structures rather than exemptions from an existing regulatory requirement.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=9 (F1,F2,F3,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10,F11), NA=2 (F4,F6). raw=F1(1)+F2(0)+F3(2)+F5(1)+F7(1)+F8(1)+F9(2)+F10(1)+F11(0)=9. max=18. pct=9/18=50.0%. Third instance this batch of the 'Manhattan-Project-scale bundling' pattern, following EO14363 [Chunk9, this stream:55.56%] and EO14369 [Chunk9, this stream:55.56%] -- see pattern log for the recurring dynamic across nuclear (Chunk7), AI/science (EO14363), space (EO14369), and now quantum (EO14413) domains.

title: Advancing Regenerative Agriculture and Strengthening American Farm Resilience

flags.justification: Directs EPA/USDA/HHS to conduct existing regulatory reviews and research programs, not unusually concentrated.

Sec 2(c) explicitly states 'Nothing in this paragraph shall be construed to direct... any regulatory action beyond current statutory requirements' -- an unusually explicit self-limiting clause constraining the order's own scope.

Bundles EPA pesticide-registration prioritization (Sec 2(a)-(b)), a cumulative-chemical-exposure research framework (Sec 2(c)), an NIH grand-prize research challenge (Sec 2(d)), and Regenerative Pilot Program funding/expansion (Sec 3) in one order.
No enforcement-against-private-party mechanism; this is a research/regulatory-review coordination order.
No notable incentive misalignment identified.
No sunset structure at issue for ongoing research/pilot programs.

Sec 2(a) explicitly requires the EPA Administrator to 'undertake all registration actions, including human health and ecological risk assessments, as expeditiously as possible as required by statute' -- preserving rather than bypassing the existing statutory risk-assessment requirements.

Sec 3's Regenerative Pilot Program expansion depends on 'producers interested in adopting regenerative practices' voluntarily participating, and Sec 2(d)'s research challenge depends on voluntary researcher participation.

No notable spillover risk identified; the order's explicit self-limiting language (Sec 2(c)) and reliance on existing statutory review processes for pesticide registration constrain the scope of unanalyzed downstream effects.

EPA, USDA, and HHS roles are reasonably delineated (EPA handles registration, USDA handles the pilot program, HHS handles research funding), consistent with the MAHA Commission's established interagency structure.
No exemption/waiver architecture at issue.

notes: Hand-tally: applicable=8 (F1,F2,F3,F5,F7,F8,F9,F10), NA=3 (F4,F6,F11). raw=F1(0)+F2(0)+F3(1)+F5(0)+F7(0)+F8(1)+F9(0)+F10(0)=2. max=16. pct=2/16=12.5%. Cites the MAHA Commission [EO14212, this stream:25.0%]. The final EO in this stream's entire corpus -- a low-scoring, research-oriented order with an unusually explicit self-limiting clause (Sec 2(c)), a fitting closing note for the batch.
